title: Privacy Pools
description: A privacy protocol that extends Tornado Cash unlinkability concept with association set providers (ASPs), allowing users to prove membership in compliant subsets without revealing their identity.

properties.name: Confidentiality
properties.notes: Privacy pools inherits the technical design from Tornado Cash which is a note-based architecture (every deposit has a secret note that allows the note owner to withdraw the funds to any address). As in Tornado Cash, it is not possible to know the total balance in the Privacy Pools protocol of a single user. Any external on-chain observer can see deposit and withdraw transactions coming to and going from the smart contracts with the public transfer amount.

properties.name: Anonymity
properties.notes: Users can deposit assets into Privacy Pools and later withdraw them, either partially or fully, without creating an on-chain link between their deposit and withdrawal addresses. This breaks the linkability between the two interacting addresses so any external on-chain observer would not be able to see any association between the addresses.

properties.name: Asset privacy
properties.notes: Users have to deposit their public tokens to the anonymity pool using a normal on-chain transaction that publicly shows which asset is being transferred. At the same time, withdrawal transactions send tokens from the smart contract to the recipient address.

properties.name: Plausible deniability
properties.notes: Anyone can see that a user is interacting with the Privacy Pools entrypoint contract to deposit or withdraw funds. As a consequence, external on-chain observers can clearly see who is using the private transfer protocol and take specific actions (e.g. flag or ban addresses).

properties.name: Number of secrets
properties.notes: The Privacy Pool user needs an Ethereum wallet (secret #1) to interact with the smart contracts and also needs to store the nullifier and secret values (secret #2) which are locally generated in the user's device in every deposit action.

properties.name: Censorship resistance
properties.notes: The Association Set Provider (ASP) is a closed framework at the moment and external ASPs cannot be configured in the current Privacy Pools contracts. In theory anyone could deploy new contracts with loose ASP controls but that would not be related to the official Privacy Pools protocol. The ASP is the core feature of the protocol because it allows users to maintain anonymity without mixing funds with malicious or sanctioned actors.

properties.name: External network dependence
properties.notes: The Association Set Provider needs to be running in other to allow deposit and withdraw function executions in the smart contracts. Hence the protocol depends on a permissioned network that authorizes smart contract interactions. If the ASP is taken down then the deposit and withdraw actions will be blocked. As stated in the censorship resistance property, there could be Privacy Pools contracts that do not rely on any ASP or rely on more trustless ASP.

properties.name: Escape hatch
properties.notes: Ragequit is a safety mechanism that allows the original depositor to publicly withdraw their funds without needing ASP approval. This operation serves as a critical backup withdrawal method which ensures the ability to withdraw user funds when the label is not approved by the ASP or its approval was revoked

properties.name: Upgradeability
properties.notes: There is no Privacy Pools or 0xbow token to support decentralized governance at the moment. There is an owner input parameter in the Entrypoint smart contract related to a Universal Upgradeable Proxy Standard (UUPS) patern.

properties.name: Client-side proving
properties.notes: A zk proof is required in other to withdraw previously deposited funds from the smart contract. The user is required to construct the withdrawal parameters and later generates a ZK proof of commitment ownership. The ZK proofs use the Groth16 proving system due to its efficiency in proof generation and on-chain proof verification.

properties.name: Third-party inspectability
properties.notes: No third-party can view the private information of the users unless the users explicitly share with a third party its note secret values. The ASP compliance mechanism checks that the transaction is not coming from suspicious source but it never grants access to unauthorized third parties to see deposit and withdraw relations.

properties.name: Implementation maturity
properties.notes: The Privacy Pools deposit contract was deployed on March 29th 2025

properties.name: Post-quantum secure
properties.notes: Privacy Pools uses zkSNARKs, based on the Groth16 proving system. Groth16 is based on Elliptic Curves Cryptography (ECC), therefore it is vulnerable to the Shor's algorithm.

properties.name: Layer of enforcement
properties.notes: The Association Set Provider blocks any suspicious withdrawal directly on the smart contracts. The user can still rage quit and withdraw to their original deposit address

properties.name: Enforcement entities
properties.notes: The Association Set Provider (ASP) administrators. There is no open-source documentation explaining how the ASP monitor and flag transactions.

properties.name: Type of compliance
properties.notes: Depending on the ASP, there could be multiple compliance mechanisms used in order to valid if a transaction is safe or not. For example one ASP might check the transaction and funds history (Know Your Transaction KYT), another ASP might verify that the interacting address does not belong to a sanctioned list (Proof of Innocence).

properties.name: Point of enforcement
properties.notes: Withdrawals to new or unrelated addresses require a valid ASP root in the zk proof. Users need to generated this proof locally and the smart contract verifies it with the on-chain ASP data. In case the withdraw transaction is not allowed, a user can use the ragequit function to withdraw funds to the initial depositor address and not to a unrelated address.

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing entity
properties.notes: Users could generate a report that links deposit and withdraw actions. There is no tool or feature at the UI to create reports but it is technically possible. These reports could be presented to authorities for tax purposes.

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing control
properties.notes: There is no mention of viewing keys in the protocol documentation. Privacy Pools is a unlinkability-only mechanism (there are no private transfers) so the concept of viewing keys might not apply to the protocol. As stated above, a user could explicitly create a report linking deposits and withdrawals to present to regulator entities.

properties.name: Cryptographic verifiability
properties.notes: It uses zkSNARK circuits written in Circom with the Groth16 proving system. All the circuits cryptography was been mathematically verified and battle tested in the ZK world.

properties.name: Open source
properties.notes: Smart contract and website code is open source. The ASP code cannot be found in the 0xbow (entity in charge of Privacy Pools) organization account

properties.name: Scalable state
properties.notes: When a user deposits funds to Privacy Pools, he needs to register a new commitment in the smart contract. All commitments are inserted in a merkle tree, therefore the tree goes infinitely as new deposit transactions are send to the protocol. At the same time withdraw function calls add nullifiers to the smart contract to prevent double spending. These nullifiers need to be stored permanently in order to mark which commitments have already been used.

properties.name: Client-side indexing
properties.notes: The private notes allow the users to withdraw their funds directly without the need to scan the blockchain for events related to the users' deposits. The official UI shows the user's privacy pools and deposit amounts. In order to do so, the interface needs to scan all deposit interactions to filter the user's transactions.

properties.name: Private state model
properties.notes: The on-chain commitments can be seen as private notes. Every deposit has a unique private note to be withdrawn in the future. In order to find the user's total balance, the user needs to sum the balance of each of his commitments (private notes).

properties.name: On-chain storage
properties.notes: Each commitment (deposit receipt) is saved in an on-chain Lean Incremental Merkle Tree (LeanIMT). The specific merkle tree was chosen due to its dynamic updates, storage and hashing optimizations.

properties.name: Access to DeFi
properties.value: Access to external, but limited choice of DeFi protocols
properties.notes: Privacy Pools implemented a yield earning module for some of its token pools. This means that some tokens can earn yield privately while been idle on the anonymity pool. There is no mention about the yield module in the documentation but there is an announcement on social media and the UI shows some pools APR percentage.

properties.name: Programmability / Generality
properties.notes: Privacy Pools v1 is similar to Tornado Cash. The main goal is to break linkability between deposit and withdraw function executions. Privacy Pools v2 is aiming to introduce private transfers inside the shielded pool. There are no plans to build programmability or generality on top of Privacy Pools in the short and medium term.

properties.name: On-chain gas cost: transfer
properties.notes: There is no private transfer inside the Privacy Pools v1 protocol. Users are only able to deposit funds using one address and withdrawing those funds to another unlinked address. The Privacy Pools v2 protocol would allow internal private transfers using a UTXO note-based architecture.
